content:
# Matthew Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_002_page_001.jpg

### The Magi from the East

1. After Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judea, during the reign of King Herod, astrologers from the east arrived in Jerusalem,

2. asking, "Where is the newborn king of the Jews? We saw his star when it rose and have come to pay him homage."

3. When King Herod heard this, he was deeply disturbed — and all of Jerusalem with him.

4. He called together all the chief priests and scholars of the law and demanded to know where the Messiah was to be born.

5. "In Bethlehem of Judea," they told him, "for the prophet wrote:

6. 'And you, Bethlehem, in the land of Judah — you are by no means the least among the leaders of Judah; for from you will come a ruler who will shepherd my people Israel.'"

7. Then Herod secretly summoned the astrologers and pressed them for the exact time the star had appeared.

8. He sent them to Bethlehem with these instructions: "Go and search carefully for the child. When you find him, report back to me so that I too may go and pay him homage."

9. After hearing the king, they set out. And there ahead of them went the star they had seen at its rising, until it stopped and stood over the place where the child was.

10. When they saw the star had stopped, they were overwhelmed with joy.

11. They entered the house and saw the child with his mother Mary, and they knelt down and paid him homage. Then they opened their treasure chests and presented him with gifts: gold, frankincense, and myrrh.

12. And having been warned in a dream not to return to Herod, they went home by a different route.

### The Flight to Egypt

13. After they had gone, an angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a dream. "Get up," he said. "Take the child and his mother and escape to Egypt. Stay there until I tell you, because Herod is going to search for the child to kill him."

14. So Joseph got up, took the child and his mother during the night, and left for Egypt,

15. where they stayed until Herod's death. This fulfilled what the Lord had spoken through the prophet: "Out of Egypt I called my son."

### The Massacre of the Innocents

16. When Herod realized that the astrologers had outwitted him, he flew into a rage. He gave orders to kill every boy in Bethlehem and its surrounding area who was two years old or younger — matching the time he had learned from the astrologers.

17. Then what was spoken through the prophet Jeremiah was fulfilled:

18. "A voice was heard in Ramah — weeping and bitter grief. Rachel weeping for her children, refusing to be comforted, because they are gone."

### The Return to Nazareth

19. After Herod died, an angel of the Lord appeared in a dream to Joseph in Egypt.

20. "Get up," he said, "take the child and his mother and go to the land of Israel, for those who were trying to take the child's life are dead."

21. So he got up, took the child and his mother, and went to the land of Israel.

22. But when he heard that Archelaus was ruling over Judea in place of his father Herod, he was afraid to go there. Warned in a dream, he withdrew to the region of Galilee,

23. and he went and settled in a town called Nazareth — fulfilling what was spoken through the prophets: "He will be called a Nazarene."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "astrologers" for μάγοι:** The traditional "wise men" sanitizes these figures. The μάγοι were Persian priestly astrologers — men who read the stars for divine messages. Calling them "astrologers" recovers the startling fact that Matthew's story has pagan star-readers as the first people to recognize the Jewish Messiah, while the Jewish religious establishment is caught off guard. The irony is intentional.

**Verse 2 — "when it rose" for ἐν τῇ ἀνατολῇ:** This phrase can mean either "in the east" (a location) or "at its rising" (an astronomical event). In context, "at its rising" (the technical astronomical sense) is more likely what astrologers would report. PNEUMA captures this with "when it rose."

**Verse 3 — "deeply disturbed" for ἐταράχθη:** Herod's disturbance is not mild surprise. The verb ταράσσω describes agitation, alarm, inner turmoil. And Matthew's note that "all of Jerusalem" shared this disturbance suggests the entire city sensed danger — when Herod was troubled, everyone had reason to fear.

**Verse 11 — "house" (not stable):** A careful note: Matthew says the magi entered a "house" (οἰκίαν) and found a "child" (παιδίον, not an infant/βρέφος). This is a different scene from Luke's manger narrative. The visit of the magi likely took place weeks or months after the birth.

**Verse 15 — Hosea 11:1:** Matthew applies "Out of Egypt I called my son" — originally referring to Israel's exodus — to Jesus. This is Matthew's typological method: Jesus recapitulates the story of Israel. Israel was God's "son" called out of Egypt; now God's Son repeats the journey.

**Verse 18 — "because they are gone":** The Greek ὅτι οὐκ εἰσίν — literally "because they are not." This is an existential statement of devastating simplicity. I render it "because they are gone" to preserve the finality without being obscure.

**Verse 23 — "Nazarene":** No single OT verse says the Messiah will be a "Nazarene." Matthew attributes this to "the prophets" (plural), suggesting a composite allusion — likely to the Hebrew נֵצֶר (netzer, "branch") in Isaiah 11:1, and possibly to the lowly reputation of Nazareth ("Can anything good come from Nazareth?" — John 1:46). The name of the town itself becomes a prophetic sign.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
